messages.content: You digest raw market-research search results into citable claim atoms for a risk-hunting pass over a market model. Extract only what the text actually says — every excerpt must be verbatim. Numeric claims are worth more than qualitative ones.

     "url": "https://www.dutchdatacenters.nl/en/publications/state-of-the-dutch-data-centers-2025/",
     "publishedDate": "2025-06-10T09:11:44.000Z",
     "text": "State of the Dutch Data Centers 2025 - Dutch Data Center Association

## Publication

# State of the Dutch Data Centers 2025

With pride, we present the anniversary edition of our annual State of the Dutch Data Centers report, this year themed: “From Boom to Bottlenecks.” This special edition marks ten years of independent data center analysis and highlights the sector’s evolution into an essential pillar of the Dutch digital economy.

The annual DDA market report is compiled based on research among members of the Dutch Data Center Association, in collaboration with Pb7 Research. The report offers a unique overview of the current state of the sector and delves into trends, investments, sustainability, regional growth, and the impact of emerging technologies such as AI.

From Boom to Bottlenecks The theme “From Boom to Bottlenecks” not only reflects the successes of the past decade, but also underscores the need for shared responsibility for the future. Data centers are no longer a niche phenomenon, they are fundamental to innovation, economic resilience, and digital sovereignty.

This publication is issued by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action as part of its public relations work. The publication is available free of charge. It is not for sale and may not be used by political parties or groups for electoral campaigning.

### Executive Summary

The growth of the digital economy and digitalisa tion is accompanied by a strong increase in the need for digital infrastructure and data centres (DCs). Data centres have thus developed into one of the fastest growing, most energy-intensive sectors in Germany. With over 2,000 data centres and an installed IT power demand of over 2,700 MW, Ger many is already the largest centre for digital infra structure in Europe. The central location, political stability, an electricity supply with above-average reliability and one of the largest internet connec tions in the world combine to make Germany a strategically advantageous place for national and international providers alike. Digitalisation targets, a growing digital economy and new technologies, such as artificial intelligence (AI), lead to growing demand for processing power, storage capacity and data transfer. This"

     "text": "### Data centers 2021

### Data center boom in Germany continues

## Cloud computing drives the growth of the data center industry and its energy consumption

#### Dr Ralph Hintemann | Simon Hinterholzer

The energy consumption of data centers continues to in crease. At 17 billion kWh, data centers consumed 6.5 % more electricity in 2021 than in 2020. The main reason for the growth in energy consumption is the expansion of cloud data centers in Germany and the associated in crease in the number of large data centers. However, traditional data centers operated by companies them selves also continue to have a high share of data center capacities in Germany. These are the results of a recent study by the Borderstep Institute on the development of the energy consumption of data centers in Germany. The growth in the energy consumption of data centers is mainly due to the increased demand for IT performance from data centers. Although servers are becoming more and more powerful and the utilization of IT systems is in creasing, especially in cloud data centers, the sharp in crease in power demand is leading to higher electricity consumption in data centers in Germany (figure 1). Of the 17 billion kWh of electricity consumed in data centers in 2021, almost 11 billion kWh went to IT components (serv ers, storage and network). Since 2010, the electricity de mand of the IT components in the data centers has thus almost doubled. In comparison, the energy consumption of the data center in"

     "text": "Data Centres Overview | CBRE Switzerland

Intelligent Investment

# Data Centres Overview

July 2, 2025 10 Minute Read

- Share on Facebook
- Share on Twitter
- Share on LinkedIn
- Email

### About This Report

Data centres play an increasingly important role in the world. The purpose-built facilities house computer systems and components, such as telecommunications and storage systems, designed to enable business processes, government services, and social interactions.

Data centres form the foundation of the digital economy, hosting the cloud, and storing our data.

However, the role of data centres is not always apparent since data centres are often located in trading estates or industrial sites formerly occupied by heavy industries.

This report aims to provide a better understanding of the fundamentals of the data centre sector. We detail the growth drivers of the asset class, the size of Europe’s largest colocation data centre markets, the type of investors involved, and key considerations of development and investment in the sector.

## Key Takeaways

>1GW

Live capacity in London, making it Europe’s largest data centre market

85%

Of take-up was recorded in FLAPD* last year *Represents the five largest colocation markets in Europe: Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin

5.8GW

Data centre capacity expected across Europe by the end of 2024

## Contacts

### Marion Vion-Dury

Associate Director - prospective and thematic studies

If ~35-40% of Germany's real installed load sits outside colocation, the colo-only denominator (CE-7 colo 3,451 MW) that geo.NL and geo.CH divide into is structurally biased toward whichever countries have lower non-colo shares — undermining the top-down capacity apportionment method used for both nodes.","nodes":["geo.NL","geo.CH"]},{"text":"Three German 'total capacity' figures disagree sharply — BMWK 2,700 MW+ (Jan 2025), DCIRD 1,955 MW (colo+enterprise>50kW), and Bitkom 2,980 MW (2025, cloud+traditional+edge) — a spread of over 1,000 MW depending on scope. This directly contradicts the search intent's premise that 'an existing/purchasable dataset... fixes the denominator scope today'; no such single consistent total-installed-load dataset was found, meaning geo.NL and geo.CH's colo-based denominator remains unvalidated against any agreed total-load standard.","nodes":["geo.NL","geo.CH"]},{"text":"For Switzerland, no new source in this search round narrows the gap already flagged in geo.CH's own provenance note between the colo-only Statista figure (274 MW) and the installed-base/self-operated-inclusive Mordor estimate (850.6 MW) or CBRE Suisse's commercial-only 340 MW;
